duration: 45-90 seconds with additional (X2-3) views of about 10-15 seconds each
description: At about 7:45 am on the morning of 16 April, I flushed up a Hermit Thrush at Crow Valley Campground in Weld County. The bird was nice enough to perch atop a brush pile about 60 feet from me. When I looked at it, its ID was clear enough, pumping its tail and making a "chack" noise. I was a bit surprised at how brown, and fairly warm brown, its back was... this caused me to pause a bit before calling Chris Nunes' attention to the bird. Before doing so, I noted that it had some buffy wash on face and upper chest, and that the flanks were washed in buff as well. The wings and back looked rather uniform, unlike the auduboni-type Hermits I am used to seeing in eastern Washington (and was expecting in CO). I double-checked to make sure the tail really was rusty, and then called to Chris, "Hermit Thrush."   He looked at the bird, and responded, "Cool." He was about 50 feet from me (not certain what his distance from the bird was.) The sun was largely to my back, and the lighting on the bird excellent. I was using Swarovski EL 10x42 binoculars. Chris quickly turned away from the bird to look at some sparrows and apparently did not hear my babblings about the buff wash at various places that were not consistent with Western Interior Hermit Thrushes.   Description The bird appeared to be within size range of Hermit Thrush, not obviously appearing at the largest or smallest ends of the spectrum for this species. The lack of comparison with other birds made further size distinctions difficult. The wings and back (and presumably crown, though I did not specifically note it) were moderately warm brown, with no hint of gray. I did not look for wingbars and could have missed weak ones. The folded primaries were rustier than remainder of wing, which was briefly noted. The tail was bright rusty, contrasting with back. Rump/uppertail covert color not specifically noted. The tail was held cocked much of the time, pumped intermittently. The eyering was moderately pronounced and white or whitish. The face had a weak buffy wash as did the upper chest. The chest was streaked/spotted in blackish brown, with individual marks seeming somewhat spot like, but loosely forming rows. Below the chest, the streaking/spotting faded into weaker spots, the extent of which was not noted. The flanks were unspotted and washed in a faint, but distinct, buff... not the pure gray I was expecting. The bird flushed, popping up for a couple more brief looks. My initial view was 45-90 seconds. Additional views were 2-3 in number, and 10-15 seconds in length, but at a distance of about 40 feet.Behaviors: The bird was nice enough to perch atop a brush pile about 60 feet from me.Call: "chack" noisePlumage: adult
